title: A Cautionary Tale: Brazil's Warning to the Global Economy on High Interest Rates
content:
The global economy offers many cautionary tales about the dangers of poor fiscal management. Argentina's inflation has shown the danger of treating a central bank like a cash machine. Italy's stagnation shows the downside of joining a currency union with high legacy debts. Britain has suffered from Brexit, which has thrown up barriers to trade with its closest neighbours. But the most timely warning for many of the world's big economies currently comes from Brazil.

Brazil has decent economic growth, an independent central bank, and its primary budget is almost balanced. Its net debts are high by emerging-market standards but relatively low compared to the rich world. However, Brazil's government must pay sky-high interest rates to service its debts. Controlling inflation has required the central bank to set short-term rates extremely high. As a result, the government will probably borrow about eight percent of its GDP just to pay its interest bill.

Closing this deficit through austerity is unlikely, as the current administration has loosened the purse strings. Unless interest rates fall significantly, public debt is guaranteed to surge. The fiscal plight of Brazil casts rich-world budgets in sharp relief. Western policymakers should consider why interest rates are so high in Brazil and heed the warning signs. Brazil's institutions, despite formal protections, remain wobbly after recent political instability.

The long-term trajectory of Brazil's budget is dire, largely due to massive spending on pensions. Without reforms, by 2050 it will spend more on pensions than much older, richer countries. These pensions are protected in the constitution, making it incredibly difficult to balance the books. This dynamic crowds out other more worthwhile government spending and investment. This diagnosis should worry the rich world, which is showing early symptoms of similar economic stagnation.
